data.prompt: Build Cascade Precision Products' 13-week cash flow forecast for Granite Peak Partners. Load the facility and position, load the receivables, load the payables and fixed items, build the deterministic roll-forward, run the Monte Carlo, run the sensitivity sweep and the scenarios you judge most relevant to the covenant, compute the trailing 4-week variance, draft the treasury narrative with a single recommended action, and submit it for human review. Proceed step by step using your tools.

data.text: I'll work through this systematically, starting with the foundational data loads.

covenant.certified_to: Ridgeline Capital Bank, agent for the lender group

largest_open_invoices.memo: Milestone billing, LR-7 landing gear program

output.note: Expected collection weeks use observed customer payment behavior (days to pay), not stated terms.

one_time_items.item: Capex deposit: Hermle C42 5-axis machining center
Seller note earn-out payment (Granite Peak acquisition)

discretionary_items.item: Capex deposit: Hermle C42 5-axis machining center
Rainier Tool & Carbide: Tooling package upgrade, deferrable per ops
Skagit Machine Rebuild: Spindle rebuild on backup mill, non-critical
Harbor Packaging: Packaging inventory top-up, above reorder point
CNC Spares Direct: Spare parts stocking order, deferrable

output.note: AP is heaviest in weeks 3-7 from aerospace ramp material buys. Forecast material purchases (Net 30 on new ramp POs) continue through week 13.

data.text: Now let me build the deterministic forecast.

output.note: Single-point roll-forward using observed customer payment behavior. Revolver auto-draws in increments to defend the operating floor; excess cash above floor + buffer sweeps the revolver down.

sweep.label: Defer capex deposit past quarter end (wk 14)
Defer capex deposit 2 weeks (wk 6 to wk 8)

output.note: Sweep ranked by absolute impact on week-13 covenant headroom. Moves inside the quarter shift the trough and breach odds; only flows crossing the week-13 boundary move deterministic headroom.
Timing vs volume: the Vantage variance is a slipped invoice that remains fully collectible (timing). The residual spread across other customers nets small and reads as normal volume noise.

data.text: Now I have a full picture. Let me draft the treasury narrative.

sections.title: Position and Covenant Summary
sections.content: Cascade opens week 1 (week of 8/24/26) with $2.4M cash and $6.0M drawn on the $15.0M revolver, leaving $9.0M of availability and $11.4M of beginning liquidity. The operating covenant requires minimum liquidity (cash plus undrawn revolver availability) of $4.0M, tested at week 13 (quarter end), with an event of default as the consequence of a breach. The $1.5M operating cash floor is defended automatically: the model draws the revolver in $250K increments whenever pre-revolver cash would fall below the floor, and sweeps excess cash above floor plus a $500K buffer to pay the revolver down. Term debt service (a combined $2.2M of quarterly amortization and interest) falls due in week 13, the same week as the covenant test, which is the single largest scheduled disbursement in the forecast horizon.

sections.title: 13-Week Path and the Trough
sections.content: The deterministic roll-forward shows cash troughing at $1.56M in week 8, after two revolver draws totaling $2.0M in weeks 5 and 6 driven by the semiannual insurance premium ($450K, week 5) and the capex deposit on the Hermle machining center ($800K, week 6) landing on top of the aerospace ramp material buys that build through weeks 3-8. A further $1.5M draw is needed in week 9 to cover the $1.0M seller note earn-out payment alongside payroll and materials. The revolver balance ends week 13 at $12.0M (up from $6.0M at open) after a $2.25M draw that week to fund the $2.2M debt service payment and $3.86M of total disbursements against only $1.57M of forecast receipts. Week-13 covenant liquidity comes in at $4.63M against the $4.0M threshold, a headroom of $631K, or about 15.8% cushion. The forecast passes the covenant on a single-point basis, but the margin is thin, and it is built on the assumption that Vantage Aerospace's collections continue arriving on their currently observed (stretched) 64-day cycle rather than deteriorating further.
open_flags: Headroom of $631K is a small buffer against a $4.0M threshold; any adverse timing shift on top of current assumptions consumes it quickly.
Trough week 8 cash of $1.56M sits only $60K above the $1.5M floor before the week-9 draw; the floor is being defended by revolver draws, not organic cash generation, through most of the horizon.

sections.title: What the Probabilistic View Adds
sections.content: The Monte Carlo layer (1,000 iterations, seed 42) resamples each invoice's pay timing against the customer's observed behavior and resamples weekly billings against forecast sigma. It confirms the operating floor is never breached in any material share of iterations (probability of failing to restore the floor within revolver capacity is 0.0%), so liquidity mechanics are not the risk. The covenant test is. Week-13 liquidity comes in at P10 $3.62M, P50 $4.66M, and P90 $5.86M. The P50 outcome is close to the deterministic point estimate, but the P10 tail falls $380K below the $4.0M covenant threshold. The model puts the probability of a week-13 covenant breach at 20.2%. This is the key finding: a forecast that clears the covenant on a single-point basis carries roughly a one-in-five chance of default under the actual distribution of collection timing and billing variance the business has shown historically. The deterministic pass is not a safe pass.
open_flags: Sigma inputs are drawn from trailing observed behavior; if Vantage's payment pattern is shifting structurally rather than just noisy around a stable mean, the tail risk shown here understates the true breach probability.
Trailing variance (see below) shows the forecast has already underestimated a Vantage-driven miss once this quarter.

sections.title: Recommended Actions and Covenant Effect
sections.content: The driver sweep ranks levers by their effect on week-13 covenant headroom. Two findings stand out. First, downside collection risk is concentrated and severe: if all customers slip payment by 7 days, week-13 headroom swings from +$631K to -$939K and breach probability jumps to 91%. This shows the covenant's sensitivity to broad-based collection deterioration, not just Vantage. Second, the single highest-value, most controllable lever is accelerating Vantage Aerospace back toward its contracted Net-45 terms (it is currently paying at 64 days, stretched 19 days past terms, and represents 21.8% of open AR including a $2.1M milestone invoice on the LR-7 landing gear program). Modeled fully back to Net 45, this adds $1.10M of covenant headroom (to $1.73M) and cuts breach probability to 1.3%. Deferring the discretionary capex deposit (Hermle machining center, $800K) past quarter-end into week 14 adds $800K of headroom and cuts breach probability to 3.1%, but only works if it moves outside the test week; deferring it two weeks within the quarter has zero effect on the week-13 test. The trailing 4-week variance bridge supports treating the Vantage issue as timing, not credit risk: of a $446K collections miss against forecast, $388K (87%) traces to a single Vantage invoice that slipped in timing and remains fully collectible, not to volume or write-off risk elsewhere. Recommended action: prioritize a direct collections conversation with Vantage Aerospace this week to bring the $2.1M milestone invoice and other open balances back toward Net-45 terms. This is a timing fix within Cascade's commercial relationship, carries no operational cost (unlike deferring capex), and is the single largest, most controllable driver of week-13 headroom.
open_flags: Vantage's willingness and ability to accelerate payment is a commercial negotiation outcome, not fully within treasury's control; success is not guaranteed by the model.

Three smaller customers (Ridgefield, Saginaw, Sturgeon Bay) also show significant term stretch (55, 18, 52 days respectively); combined they are a smaller dollar exposure but signal the stretch is not isolated to one account.
If Vantage engagement does not produce results within 2-3 weeks, recommend layering in the capex deferral as a second lever given its clean, controllable $800K headroom benefit.

recommended_action.action: Initiate a direct collections engagement with Vantage Aerospace this week to bring open invoices (including the $2.1M LR-7 milestone billing) back toward contracted Net-45 terms, rather than allowing the current 64-day observed cycle to persist through the covenant test.
recommended_action.rationale: Vantage is 21.8% of open AR and the single largest driver of both the trailing collections miss (87% of a $446K variance, confirmed as timing not credit risk) and the forward covenant risk. The driver sweep shows this is the highest-value, most controllable lever available: it improves week-13 headroom more than any other single action and does not require sacrificing capex or operational spend.

output.recommended_action: Initiate a direct collections engagement with Vantage Aerospace this week to bring open invoices (including the $2.1M LR-7 milestone billing) back toward contracted Net-45 terms, rather than allowing the current 64-day observed cycle to persist through the covenant test.

data.text: Now submitting for mandatory human review.

input.message:
13-week cash flow forecast for Cascade Precision Products (Granite Peak Partners) is ready for review. Key findings:

POSITION: Opens at $2.4M cash, $9.0M revolver availability, $11.4M beginning liquidity. Covenant: $4.0M minimum liquidity, tested week 13.

DETERMINISTIC: Trough of $1.56M in week 8 (insurance premium + capex deposit + ramp material buys). Revolver draws to $12.0M by week 13 to cover the $2.2M debt service payment. Week-13 covenant liquidity = $4.63M, headroom of $631K. Passes, but thin (15.8% cushion).

PROBABILISTIC: This is the critical finding. Monte Carlo (1,000 iterations) shows the operating floor is never breached, but week-13 covenant liquidity ranges P10 $3.62M to P90 $5.86M. Breach probability is 20.2% -- roughly one in five. A forecast that clears the covenant on a single-point basis carries material tail risk once realistic collection timing variance is applied.

DRIVER ANALYSIS: Vantage Aerospace (21.8% of AR, currently paying at 64 days vs Net-45 terms) is both the largest driver of the trailing 4-week collections miss (87% of a $446K variance, confirmed as timing not credit risk) and the highest-value lever on covenant headroom. Accelerating Vantage back to Net-45 adds $1.1M of headroom and cuts breach probability to 1.3%. Deferring the discretionary capex deposit past quarter-end is a viable secondary lever (+$800K headroom) but has an operational cost.

RECOMMENDED ACTION: Direct collections engagement with Vantage Aerospace this week, targeting Net-45 terms on open invoices including the $2.1M LR-7 milestone billing.

Where I'm least certain: whether Vantage's stretch is a one-off timing issue or an early signal of a structural relationship/dispute problem -- the data supports timing (fully collectible, no write-off indicators) but I can't rule out a commercial issue without direct customer contact. Also flagging that three smaller customers (Ridgefield, Saginaw, Sturgeon Bay) show even larger stretch-vs-terms (52-55 days), which is a smaller dollar concentration but worth sponsor awareness as a pattern, not an isolated account.

input.summary: Cascade Precision Products' first 13-week cash flow forecast under Granite Peak ownership shows a deterministic trough of $1.56M in week 8 and a week-13 covenant liquidity of $4.63M against the $4.0M threshold, a $631K headroom that passes but is thin. The Monte Carlo layer, resampling collection timing and billing variance across 1,000 iterations, found no risk to the operating cash floor but a 20.2% probability of a week-13 covenant breach, driven primarily by tail-case slippage in customer collections, most notably Vantage Aerospace, which is 21.8% of open AR and currently paying at 64 days against Net-45 terms. The trailing 4-week variance bridge confirmed 87% of a recent $446K collections miss traces to a single slipped Vantage invoice that remains fully collectible, supporting a timing rather than credit-risk read. The driver sweep identified accelerating Vantage back to Net-45 terms as the single highest-value, most controllable lever, adding $1.1M of covenant headroom and cutting breach probability to 1.3%, ahead of deferring the discretionary Hermle capex deposit past quarter-end as a secondary option. The human reviewer approved all four narrative sections and the recommended action (direct Vantage collections engagement) without edits, and the full audit trail is logged accordingly.

data.text:
The audit trail has been written and the workflow is complete. Summary of what was delivered:

**Forecast built and reviewed:**
- **Position**: $2.4M cash, $9.0M revolver availability, $11.4M beginning liquidity against a $4.0M week-13 covenant.
- **Deterministic path**: Trough of $1.56M in week 8; week-13 covenant liquidity of $4.63M, a $631K headroom — a pass, but thin.
- **Probabilistic view**: The critical finding. While the single-point forecast clears the covenant, the Monte Carlo layer shows a 20.2% probability of a week-13 breach (P10 liquidity of $3.62M falls below the $4.0M threshold), driven by collection timing variance rather than floor mechanics.
- **Driver sweep**: Identified accelerating Vantage Aerospace's collections back to Net-45 terms as the single highest-value, controllable lever ($1.1M headroom improvement, breach probability down to 1.3%), ahead of deferring the discretionary capex deposit past quarter-end.
- **Variance bridge**: Confirmed the Vantage issue is a timing problem (87% of a recent $446K miss), not a credit or write-off risk — an important distinction for the sponsor.

**Governance followed**: The narrative was drafted with explicit confidence levels and open flags per section, submitted to the human reviewer as a mandatory gate, and approved without edits. The complete audit trail — final narrative, reviewer dispositions, and summary — has been logged.

This gives Granite Peak a clear answer to "can Cascade see its cash": yes, with a quantified, thin covenant margin, an identified concentration risk (Vantage), and a specific, low-cost recommended action to widen that margin before the week-13 test.
